narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know hearing that your law school application was rejected because our model predicts a 47% chance of not passing the bar exam feels like a gut punch—it's not just numbers, it's your dream on the line, and I'm here to walk you through why it landed this way, like sharing the story behind a tough family decision. Picture the model looking at your journey so far: your Year 3 grades placed you in the 2nd decile at school, which stood out as the biggest signal pulling toward this prediction, suggesting the rigors of upper-level courses have been an uphill battle. That teamed up with your LSAT score of 20.7, the next strongest factor, painting a picture of challenges in the kind of high-stakes reasoning the bar demands. Your Year 1 decile of 3 didn't help either, showing this wasn't a one-off struggle but a consistent pattern in a competitive environment. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket added another layer, as the model sees it correlating with extra hurdles in accessing resources that often tip the scales for bar success. Your Asian background, while a source of strength for so many, nudged the outlook toward failure in this mix, alongside your undergraduate GPA of 2.4, which whispered doubts about foundational academic stamina. Even opting for part-time status and being male wove in as quieter but still influential threads, all combining to form a story of higher risk rather than the smoother path the model favors for passage. It's not that you're incapable—far from it—but this specific blend of your experiences signaled caution to us. The good news? You're early enough to target law head-on with focused improvements, like boosting those deciles or prepping that LSAT further. You've got the drive; now channel it to rewrite this story. We're rooting for your comeback.
